summary: DHH contrasts the visible independence of children in Copenhagen with the heavily supervised childhood experience in America. He observes that Danish kids routinely take public transit alone, run errands, and play unsupervised from young ages — freedoms that have largely vanished for American children. Drawing on Jonathan Haidt's work, he links this loss of autonomy to broader social problems in the US. He argues that Denmark's example should be more relatable to Americans than Japan's, since the societies share many values, and urges American parents to actively seek opportunities to grant their children independence.
key_points: Danish children have visible autonomy in daily life — taking metro alone, biking in traffic, running errands — that would be unthinkable for most American kids
America actually has slightly more children per capita than Denmark, but kids are far less visible in American cities because their lives are so heavily supervised
This level of childhood independence was normal in America in the 1980s but has been systematically eroded
Denmark's example should be more actionable for Americans than Japan's because the societies are culturally similar
The loss of childhood autonomy may contribute to growing authoritarianism and social problems, per Jonathan Haidt's research
money_quotes: All these observations stick out, but none trigger the culture shock like seeing kids everywhere.
It's the fact that this prevalence of kids everywhere means they have real autonomy and independence. Freedoms that have been rapidly disappearing for many American kids.
In the US, virtually all interactions outside of school for our elementary school kids were not just scheduled, but supervised and chaperone by adults.
There's such a glow of accomplishment on the face of our boys when they push another boundary of independence.
You'd immediately be pursued by social services or the police in the US if you let a three-year old run around the city by themselves.
It's not a force of nature that's continually curtailing the autonomy and independence of American kids.
What gift of independence might I be able to bestow my kid today? I guarantee that the value of receiving such gifts is universal.
key_insight: The disappearance of childhood independence in America is not inevitable but a cultural choice, and Denmark demonstrates that similar Western societies can preserve children's autonomy with positive results.
